coredata.dc:title: Influence of ultrasound power on acoustic streaming and micro-bubbles formations in a low frequency sono-reactor: Mathematical and 3D computational simulation
coredata.prism:copyright: Copyright © 2014 Elsevier B.V. All rights reserved.
coredata.dc:creator: Sajjadi, Baharak
Raman, Abdul Aziz Abdul
Ibrahim, Shaliza
coredata.dc:description: This paper aims at investigating the influence of ultrasound power amplitude on liquid behaviour in a low-frequency (24kHz) sono-reactor. Three types of analysis were employed: (i) mechanical analysis of micro-bubbles formation and their activities/characteristics using mathematical modelling. (ii) Numerical analysis of acoustic streaming, fluid flow pattern, volume fraction of micro-bubbles and turbulence using 3D CFD simulation. (iii) Practical analysis of fluid flow pattern and acoustic streaming under ultrasound irradiation using Particle Image Velocimetry (PIV). In mathematical modelling, a lone micro bubble generated under power ultrasound irradiation was mechanistically analysed. Its characteristics were illustrated as a function of bubble radius, internal temperature and pressure (hot spot conditions) and oscillation (pulsation) velocity. The results showed that ultrasound power significantly affected the conditions of hotspots and bubbles oscillation velocity. From the CFD results, it was observed that the total volume of the micro-bubbles increased by about 4.95% with each 100W-increase in power amplitude. Furthermore, velocity of acoustic streaming increased from 29 to 119cm/s as power increased, which was in good agreement with the PIV analysis.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Ultrasound
Micro bubbles
Acoustic streaming
Computational Fluid Dynamics (CFD)
Particle Image Velocimetry (PIV)

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Geometry, boundary conditions and numerical solution of equations
Micro bubbles generations, dynamics and activities under ultrasound irradiation
Liquid velocity and flow pattern under ultrasound irradiation

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Experimental setup used for ultrasound assisted transesterification.
Distribution of pressure pulses within the system under ultrasound power of 100W. (a) τ/4, (b) 2τ/4, (c) 3τ/4 and (d) 4τ/4.
Distribution of cavitation bubbles within the system under ultrasound power of 400W.

Distribution of cavitation bubbles in ultrasonicator: (a) at different liquid elevation with power input of 400W, (b) under different ultrasound power amplitude. From the bottom: (a) − h =7cm, .... h =6.6cm, --- h =5cm, --- h =4cm, -.-.-. h =3cm, −−− h =2cm, −.−.− h =1cm. (b) − power 100W, h =6.6cm, .... power 200W h =6.6cm, --- power 300W h =6.6cm, --- power 400W h =6.6cm.

Simulation results of cavitation bubble characteristics, (a) radius (R/R 0), (b) oscillatory velocity, (c) temperature and (d) pressure; under ultrasound power of −−− 100W, --- 200W, −−− 300W, − •• − •• − 400W.
Liquid flow pattern induced by ultrasound irradiation presented by (1) PIV and (2) CFD, under power amplitude of (a) 100W, (b) 200W, (c) 300W and (d) 400W.

Distribution of velocity magnitude (a) in different elevation of the liquid and (b) under different power input. (a) Under ultrasound with power of 400W. From the bottom: − h =7cm, .... h =6cm, --- h =5cm, --- h =4cm, -.-.-. h=3cm, −−− h =2cm. (b) − h=5cm 400W, .... h =4cm 400W, --- h =5cm 300W, --- h =4cm 300W, -.-.-. h =5cm 200W, −−− h =4cm 200W, −.−.− h =5cm 100W, −..−..− h =4cm 100W.
Distribution of turbulence intensity in the ultrasound irradiation with power of 400W.

Distribution of turbulence intensity under the ultrasound irradiation − h =5cm 400W, .... h =5cm, 300W, --- h =5cm 200W, --- h =5cm 100W, -.-.-. h =3.5cm 400W, −−− h =3.5cm 300W, −.−.− h =3.5cm 200W, −..−..− h =3.5cm 100W.

head.ce:title: Influence of ultrasound power on acoustic streaming and micro-bubbles formations in a low frequency sono-reactor: Mathematical and 3D computational simulation

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemical Engineering, Faculty of Engineering, University of Malaya, 50603 Kuala Lumpur, Malaysia

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: This paper aims at investigating the influence of ultrasound power amplitude on liquid behaviour in a low-frequency (24kHz) sono-reactor. Three types of analysis were employed: (i) mechanical analysis of micro-bubbles formation and their activities/characteristics using mathematical modelling. (ii) Numerical analysis of acoustic streaming, fluid flow pattern, volume fraction of micro-bubbles and turbulence using 3D CFD simulation. (iii) Practical analysis of fluid flow pattern and acoustic streaming under ultrasound irradiation using Particle Image Velocimetry (PIV). In mathematical modelling, a lone micro bubble generated under power ultrasound irradiation was mechanistically analysed. Its characteristics were illustrated as a function of bubble radius, internal temperature and pressure (hot spot conditions) and oscillation (pulsation) velocity. The results showed that ultrasound power significantly affected the conditions of hotspots and bubbles oscillation velocity. From the CFD results, it was observed that the total volume of the micro-bubbles increased by about 4.95% with each 100W-increase in power amplitude. Furthermore, velocity of acoustic streaming increased from 29 to 119cm/s as power increased, which was in good agreement with the PIV analysis.

ce:section.ce:para: Use of ultrasound energy (a combination of sinusoidal pressure waves with a frequency of >20kHz) in a system affects the pressure balance within the media, producing a large number of microbubbles and micro jets in micro scale and creating a special fluid flow pattern in macro scale. Furthermore, a large number of localized over-heated regions named as hotspots are produced by vigorous collapses of these bubbles. Therefore, regions with pressure of about 1000atm and temperature of 10,000K are generated within only a microsecond. Generally, the formation, growth, and implosive collapse of microbubbles are known cavitation. These phenomena may increase mixing intensity and mass transfer within the system. Hence, ultrasound is frequently and successfully employed in liquid mixing, wastewater treatment, extraction, crystallization, emulsification, chemical reactions and etc. Many researchers have also studied such applications. However, most of the previous researches have only focused on modality, quality of heat propagation and temperature profile within the system. Analytical or simulation analysis of ultrasound irradiation and acoustic streaming have rarely been investigated [1–3] and this is one of the major restrictions in optimization of ultrasound applications in industries.

The numerical simulation of acoustic streaming was initiated and accomplished by Rayleigh and Nyborg [4] who indicated that acoustic streaming could be a second-order nonlinear result of acoustic wave propagation. They calculated the streaming through Navier–Stokes equation and neglected the inertia term (convective acceleration term). Later, Lighthill established a model to show that acoustic streaming took the form of inertia dominated turbulent jet at powers above 4×10−4 W. In the Lighthill [5] model, the horn tip is an inlet where all the acoustic energy absorbed by the liquid is converted into turbulent motion or jet. Besides, Lighthill [6] reported that neglecting the inertia term of the Navier–Stokes equation was true only for “creeping motions” where there were very slow flows with Reynolds numbers <1 and low power.

Based on the theory presented by Lighthill on the Navier–Stokes and κ–ε turbulent equations, Trujillo et al. [7] tried to simulate the acoustically induced ultrasound streaming at powers higher than or equal to 30W. Nastac [8] also developed an ultrasound modeling approach to predict the acoustic streaming and ultrasonic cavitation. His approach was based on the numerical solution of Lighthills’s acoustic analogy, fluid flow and heat transfer equations along with mesoscopic modeling of grain structure. He applied this model to control the solidification microstructure and improve the quality of cast ingots under ultrasound irradiation. He reported that the predicted acoustic streaming was strong while the ultrasonic cavitation was relatively small and meaningless in solidification process.

The velocity potential of the far-field and near-field of these micro bubbles also named as cavitation bubbles should be modified in order to improve the Rayleigh model. This is considered in the Rayleigh–Plesset model using radial sound wave emitted from the bubbles. Niazi et al. [9] focused on pressure and temperature distribution in acoustic cavitation based on the Rayleigh–Plesset theory. Wave propagation was assumed linear and shear stress was ignored in their simulation. Finally, they successfully predicted the collapse temperature (3200K) and pressure (3000atm) in the active cavitation zones of a liquid bulk.

However, sound radiation enhances the order of the Rayleigh–Plesset equation. Therefore, Rayleigh–Plesset model has an unstable spurious solution which grows exponentially in time and causes numerical errors. In order to eliminate this problem, Keller and Miksis suggested calculating
d
2
/
dt
2
R
2
R
̇
by the Rayleigh equation and inserting the value into the Rayleigh–Plesset equation. A more complex model for characterizing bubble dynamics using the Rayleigh–Plesset equation was proposed by van Wijngaarden [10]. This model is able to represent transient shock waves in a bubbly mixture by capturing inertial effects. Commander and Prosperetti [11] established an equation for wave propagation in a bubbly liquid by adding a nonlinear term to the model presented by Wijngaarden [10] to account for the damping effect of bubbles caused by thermal, viscous and acoustic effects. Jamshidi et al. [12] added three different assumptions to the model in order to demonstrate the sensitivity of the Wijngaarden model: (i) a linear wave with a constant volume fraction of bubbles; (ii) a linear wave without consideration of bubbles; and (iii) a linear wave with an assumed linear relationship between the acoustic pressure amplitude and the volume fraction of bubbles. They reported that acoustic excitation/cavitation should be utilized as a source term for the momentum transfer since micro bubbles significantly affected wave propagation. This analytical method involved homogenous/inhomogeneous Helmholtz equation, as employed by the other researchers who had focused on the effects of system hydrodynamic on acoustic streaming in low/high frequency low-power sonoreactors [13,14]. In two other works, Rahimi et al. [15] and Liu et al. [16] used a similar model to investigate acoustic streaming in a high-frequency sono-reactor and an airlift sonobioreactor by using CFD simulation. Both substituted the plane form of sound pressure waves using the Helmholtz equation with compressible Navier stocks equations along with the Rayleigh–Plesset equation. Recently, Jiao et al. [17] have investigated the influence of ultrasound irradiation on mass transfer coefficient by using the same model along with the inhomogeneous Helmholtz equation. They did not report any original graph from their simulation results. However, they reported that mass transfer coefficient increased with temperature, ultrasound power and frequency but decreased with decreasing transducer diameters and distance between the reactors and ultrasound sources.
However, analysis of ultrasonic wave distribution in liquid media and generation of micro bubbles are challenging since not all phenomena are quantified and well understood [10].
Generally, in mathematical modelling of the mechanic of the bubble’s interior, different approaches and corresponding models can be employed.

Assuming that there is a full-compressible gas dynamic in the bubble, the motion of gas should be described by the Navier–Stokes equation and equations of mass and energy. The boundary conditions at the moving bubble wall r = R(t) can be predicted by either Rayleigh–Plesset equation or fluid-dynamic equations. According to the aforementioned discussion, three analytical approaches can be employed in which the bubbles are considered spherically symmetric:
i.
Inviscid models, which do not predict the effects of total energy balance of the bubble.
ii.
Dissipative models, which present some estimation of heat conduction and energy-loss.
iii.
Dissipative models which include phase change, in which the water vapour inside the bubble plays an important role in regulating the bubble heat transfer across the bubble wall.

However, when the sophisticated interplay of physical effects inside a bubble is involved, spatial inhomogeneities inside the bubble are not very pronounced. Therefore, in the other approach, it is assumed that there is a uniform bubble interior and thus Naviers–Stokes equations are not used. Based on this explanation, two types of modelling can be employed:
i.
Homogeneous van der Waals gas without heat and mass exchange. In this approach, an adiabatic equation of state is considered for bubble interior and the corresponding equation of temperature.
ii.
Homogeneous van der Waals gas with heat and mass exchange. In this approach, a more complex model that considers mass and heat exchange between the bubble and media is used. For example, Toegel model [18] and Yasui model [19] have been developed based on this approach. The former is a diffusion limited model while the latter is a condensation-based model.

In this study, the former approach “Homogeneous van der Waals gas without heat and mass exchange”, was used to estimate the physical specifications inside the bubble and the bubble hydrodynamic. Accordingly, the same approach was employed in CFD simulation since it is common to eliminate the diffusion into and out of the cavity by considering only hydrodynamic [20–22]. Besides, a full-cavitation model was used to approximate the phase exchange. The purpose of this study is to analyze the effect of ultrasound power induced by a low-frequency sono-reactor on behavior of a system. Three analytical methods were employed: (i) using the Keller-Miksis model to investigate the microbubbles activities, dynamics and their characteristics; (ii) using the Rayleigh–Plesset equation along with the Singhal model [23] in the CFD simulation to study the effect of ultrasound irradiation on volume fraction of micro-bubbles, acoustic streaming characteristics and fluid flow pattern and; (iii) using PIV analysis to practically evaluate the findings and validate the simulation results. In this study, the word “acoustic streaming” refers to the high-speed fluid flow generated by ultrasound that is initiated from the surface of transducer toward the bottom of the vessel, exerting an impinging force on the liquid and pushing it to circulate in the vessel.

Fig. 1 shows the experimental setup in this study which consisted of an ultrasonic processor (Heilscher/UP 400s), a horn (Horn H22) and an electronic thermometer. The ultrasonic processor generated longitudinal mechanical vibrations through electrical excitation (reversed piezoelectric effect) with a frequency of 24kHz and output power between 100 and 400W. The vibrations were amplified by a sonotrode fitted to a horn (formed as a
λ / 2
vibrator) and transferred via its end to the medium.

The fluid motion under ultrasound irradiation was measured using Particle Image Velocimetry (PIV) in two dimensions (Dantec Dynamics, Denmark) along with the software package of Flow Manager v40.00 (80S30). The PIV system employed was a dual-power system with 135-15 Laser, 2×135mJ at 15Hz (pulse length of 2–9ns, and timing jitter of <0.5ns) and two double-shutter cameras with a resolution of 2048×2058pixels (81C92 Flow Sense EO 4M camera, 16Hz C-mount). The images were analyzed using 80N96 high performance 3D processor (precision T7500). Frame straddling allowed a minimum time of 2–9ns between the frames. Furthermore, each camera was equipped with an orange optical filter to clear the images from undesired reflections. The liquid bulk was seeded with fluorescent particles with a diameter of 50μm. These particles emit orange light (∼640nm) and absorb laser beam with a wavelength of 532nm. The obtained images were normalized to improve their contrast and minimize the effect of intensity differences over the area of the light sheet. In the experiments, the cameras were aligned to the laser sheet at a degree of π/4. Water was used as the Newtonian fluid and the experiments were performed under atmospheric pressure and ambient temperature. The analytical diagram is presented in Fig. 2 .

In mathematical modelling, a lone micro bubble generated under power ultrasound irradiation was mechanistically analysed. Its characteristics were illustrated as a function of bubble radius, internal temperature and pressure (hot spot conditions) and oscillation (pulsation) velocity. Neither breakage nor coalescence was considered since the focus was on characteristics of a lone bubble which was generated under ultrasound irradiation with frequency of 24kHz and power range of 100–400W.

In this study, the Keller–Miksis Equation (KME) was used to describe the radial dynamics of the bubble. In this model, liquid compressibility is taken into account and radiation dumping is considered through the time derivative of gas pressure [24].

where γ is the polytrophic component which represents the ratio between heat diffusion and heat advection. It also shows the possibility of isothermal behaviour of the bubble interior. h denotes the van der Waals hard core radius (=R 0/8.86).

The internal temperature of bubbles, assumed as a uniform profile, is calculated from the van der Waals process equation along with a diffusive heat loss term:

where Tb (t) and T0 are the bubble and ambient temperature, respectively.

The equations in the bubble dynamics model were solved simultaneously using the well-known Runge–Kutta adaptive step size method. Programming codes were generated in MATLAB (Version 7.12, Mathworks, Natick, MA).

In the CFD simulation of ultrasonicator, two phases of liquid and gas were considered by the “Mixture model”. The continuity equation for the mixture of different phases (k) is stated as:

This equation shows that the mass transfer rate does not only depend on the vapour density, but it is a function of mixture density and liquid density. Besides, it is directly derived from phase volume fraction equations and thus it accurately illustrates the mass transfer between liquid and vapour phases (liquid evaporation or bubble growth) in the cavitation. Eq. (16) is not only used to estimate bubble growth but also bubble collapse by using the absolute value of the pressure difference and considering the term on the right as a sink term. Besides, in Eq. (16) the bubble pressure is the same as the saturation vapor pressure in the absence of viscous damping, mass transport and dissolved gases; therefore
P

p
sat
.

In the case that vapour mass fraction is the dependent variable in the transport equation; Singhal [23] has developed a model based on Eq. (16). In this model, vapour transport equation due to evaporation/condensation is governed by the vapour volume fraction transport equation:

As mentioned earlier, the bubbles dynamic and cavitation were considered through the Rayleigh–Plesset model and Singhal model, respectively in this study.

The conservation of momentum is expressed by Navier–Stokes equations with the following general form.
(20)

ij

Liquid is assumed as an incompressible flow of a Newtonian fluid with constant viscosity in the general form of the Navier–Stokes equations. However, this assumption rules out the possible occurrence of shock or sound waves. Description of ultrasound phenomena requires another presentation form of the momentum equation that takes into account fluid compressibility. Furthermore, cavitation phenomena should also be considered. Hence, the following form of Navier–Stokes which consists of both phase volume fraction and viscosity term was employed in this study.
(21)

In this equation, μ, g and F are fluid viscosity, gravity acceleration and interfacial momentum exchange terms, respectively.

In most of the ultrasound applications, acoustic energy absorbed by the liquid bulk is converted into turbulent motion. Therefore, fluid flow is turbulent instead of laminar. In this study, the standard
k - ε
model was employed to describe the turbulent flow behaviour in the sonoreactor.

In this model, the instantaneous velocity term in the Navier–Stokes equation is divided into the fluctuating and averaged part
u =

.

ce:list-item.ce:para: As discussed earlier, ultrasound waves are sinusoidal pressure waves and their temporal behaviour is normally based on its frequency. Hence, the computational time-domain should be less than wave frequency in CFD simulation of acoustic phenomena in liquid bulk or any other media. Otherwise, it is impossible to investigate the unsteady behavior of acoustic wave. Therefore, a transient solution with a time step of 1e−5 sec was considered in this study.

Isothermal condition was assumed in the whole fluid domain due to several reasons: (i) sono-reactor is surrounded with water jacket in order to control the temperature; (ii) to reduce computational demand as temperature influences viscosity and density.
The slip velocity which is defined as the relative velocity between the gas and liquid phase was ignored.
The gas phase was considered of comprising spherical bubbles of the same size. All phases were treated as interpenetrating continua.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Geometry, boundary conditions and numerical solution of equations
ce:section.ce:para: Eqs. (8)–(23) were numerically solved using FLUENT (Version 6.2). All details of the boundary conditions are presented in Fig. 3 . The volume fraction of vapour was considered as zero in all domains and boundaries. Besides, the residuals for all predicted parameters were considered to be less than 10−5. Furthermore, 499,758 tetrahedral elements with 93,770 nodes and minimum element quality of 1.5mm were considered. The quality of the mesh element is shown in the same figure. The Finite Volume Method (FVM) which is a common approach in CFD was applied to solve the governing equations over the discrete control volumes [27,28]. Besides, Semi-Implicit Method for Pressure-Linked algorithm (SIMPLE) along with the upwind first-order scheme were used for discretization of pressure–velocity coupling equations.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Micro bubbles generations, dynamics and activities under ultrasound irradiation
ce:section.ce:para: As mentioned earlier, ultrasound waves passed through the liquid through distribution of periodical pressure waves. Fig. 4 presents the pressure changes contours over the transducer and in the liquid during a complete cycle. It was observed that the pressure inside the liquid changed periodically. Microbubbles are generated when the pressure amplitude of the ultrasound waves is larger than the ambient pressure. In this condition, the pressure in the liquid medium becomes instantaneously negative, putting the liquid under tension. The liquid then breaks apart and generates unstable cavitation bubbles when there is sufficient tension [29]. Therefore, the maximum volume fraction of microbubbles was observed in the vicinity of ultrasound transducer imposed by greater ultrasound power and acoustic pressure, as illustrated in Fig. 5 .

The volume fraction of microbubbles in different elevations of ultrasonoreactor is also shown in Fig. 6 (a). At first, it should be mentioned that the first line is with the distance of 3mm below transducer. Therefore, two peaks were highlighted in regions exactly below the transducer, presenting the maximum volume fraction of micro bubbles. It was due to the pressure of ultrasound waves which pushed the bubbles aside although a uniform volume of bubbles was generated by the transducer. Therefore, spreading of bubbles occurred from the edges of corners to the transducer. This profile is also indicated in Fig. 5.

Besides, the diameter and number of micro bubbles increased with ultrasound amplitude and therefore their volume fraction increased, as observed in Fig. 6(b). According to the CFD simulation results, the total volume of the bubbles increased by about 4.95% with each 100W-increase in power amplitude. However, distribution of the bubbles within the media increased by only 0.6%.

According to the mechanistical modelling (Fig. 7 a), micro bubbles grew up to almost 3.76 times of their initial radius till the moment of compression phase under power amplitude of 100W. The bubbles reached a radius of 4.53 times greater than their initial radius when the ultrasound power rose to about 400W. It was attributed to the expansion rate of the micro bubbles which is more prominent at higher power compared to lower power. Therefore, the maximum radius was observed at 400W.

Moreover, ultrasound power also affected oscillation velocity. As observed, the value of oscillation velocity increased from 8.76cm/s to 13.65cm/s with increased power amplitude (Fig. 7b). This indicated the prominent role of ultrasound and cavitation on increasing the mixing intensity.

Analysis of temperature and pressure of micro bubbles clarified the hotspot conditions. As observed in (Fig. 7c and d), higher ultrasound amplitude prominently increased both temperature and pressure of microbubbles. It was attributed to greater energy transfer within the system which resulted in higher energy accumulation within the bubbles. Mathematical simulation showed that both temperature and pressure increased from 2687 to 3571K and 268 to 510bar, respectively with increased ultrasound power.
The details of the simulation results are also summarized in (Table 1 ).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Liquid velocity and flow pattern under ultrasound irradiation
ce:section.ce:para: The PIV results at four different power amplitudes (100, 200, 300, 400W) are presented in Figs. 8 and 1(a–d). An intense axial flow just under the transducer, marked by a high density of vertical vectors, was clearly visible. Besides, the presence of a recirculation flow was revealed by the vectors directed diagonally against the transducer near the reactor walls. The same axial flow pattern was also established by the CFD simulation which is presented in Figs. 8 and 2(a–d).

As observed, an acoustic streaming was generated under ultrasound irradiation, which pushed the fluid downward and then upward consequently. On the other hand, an axial flow was generated by the sonoreactor in downward direction from the transducer towards the top of the tank. The transducer generated a uniform circulation of fluid that covered all regions. This type of flow pattern inhibits accumulation of denser liquid at the bottom or less dense liquid on the top, making it the most suitable for mixing of liquids with different densities. Moreover, the flow pattern is stable and does not change with power input.

The variations in averaged velocities with vessel heights for ultrasound power of 400W are presented in Fig. 9 (a). As observed, the highest velocity was predicted for regions closer to the transducer which gave the value of 145cm/s. However, the velocity decreased to almost 30cm/s at regions near the bottom of the ultrasonicator. The direction of the acoustic streaming then changed towards the top of the vessel while its velocity in the regions beside the wall decreased to almost 0.24 of the maximum velocity observed at the vessel centre. Moreover, the lowest velocity magnitude under ultrasound irradiation was observed near the free surface of the vessel with values between 13 and 21cm/s.

Fig. 9(b) presents the velocity profiles corresponding to the planes with distance of 4 and 5cm from the bottom of the ultrasonicator under different power inputs. As observed, the velocity of the acoustic streaming increased from 29cm/s to 1.19m/s as the power amplitude increased from 100 to 400W at 4cm from the bottom of the ultrasonicator. The value increased from 43cm/s to almost 145cm/s in regions exactly under the transducer. Therefore, the velocity under ultrasound irradiation was prominently affected by power input.

According to the PIV results, the velocity increased with power amplitude until the maximum jet-flow velocity of about 1.23m/s in the system. Besides, the velocity at the highest power (400W) was almost 5 times greater than that of the lowest power which was about 24cm/s. The recirculation flow also increased slightly with power. Consequently, velocity behaved in a similar manner as established by the CFD simulation, in which velocity also increased with power (Fig. 8).

The distribution of turbulence kinetic energy in the ultrasonicator is presented in Figs. 10 and 11
. Based on the fluid flow patterns and pressure profiles, the turbulence kinetic energy was at the maximum level below the transducer due to the presence of acoustic streaming while the lowest turbulence kinetic energy was found in the regions far from the transducer. Furthermore, the values of turbulent kinetic energy were critically affected by the power input. It was observed that the kinetic energy increased from 96(%) to 138(%) as the power amplitude increased from 100 to 400W.

In the present study, the effects of ultrasound power on cavitation phenomena and liquid behavior were investigated in a low-frequency/high-power sonicator. The study focused on microbubble generation, characteristics and activities, jet flow behavior induced by ultrasound and fluid flow pattern. Three analytical methods were employed which included mathematical modeling, CFD simulation and PIV analysis. It was observed from mathematical analysis that increase in the power amplitude from 100 to 400W imposed a great influence on hotspot conditions (temperature and pressure of microbubble) and oscillation velocity. Moreover, greater power amplitude generated bigger bubbles though it did not affect their life cycle. According to the CFD simulation results, the total volume of the bubbles increased by about 4.95% with each 100W-increase in power amplitude. In terms of liquid behavior, generation of acoustic streaming which pushed the fluid downward and upward consequently under ultrasound irradiation was clearly observed in both analytical methods (CFD and PIV). According to CFD results, the velocity of acoustic streaming increased from 29cm/s to 1.19m/s as the power amplitude increased from 100 to 400W, which was in great agreement with the results obtained by the PIV.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors are grateful to the University of Malaya High Impact Research Grant (HIR-MOHE-D000038-16001) from the Ministry of Higher Education Malaysia and University of Malaya Bright Spark Unit which financially supported this work.
